cards.front: A 65-year-old smoker presents with painless hematuria. What is the most likely diagnosis and next step?
cards.back: Most likely: Bladder cancer (transitional cell carcinoma). Next step: Cystoscopy with biopsy.
cards.notes: Risk factors: smoking (#1), aniline dyes, cyclophosphamide. Painless hematuria in adults = bladder cancer until proven otherwise.

cards.front: What is the first-line treatment for community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in an outpatient with no comorbidities?
cards.back: Amoxicillin or doxycycline (or a macrolide such as azithromycin if local resistance is low).
cards.notes: With comorbidities: respiratory fluoroquinolone OR β-lactam + macrolide. Inpatient: same as comorbidities regimen.

cards.front: What are the Ottawa Ankle Rules?
cards.back: X-ray is indicated if: 1) Bone tenderness at posterior edge of distal 6 cm of tibia or fibula (malleoli), 2) Bone tenderness at base of 5th metatarsal or navicular, 3) Inability to bear weight (4 steps) immediately and in ED.
cards.notes: High sensitivity (~98%) for ruling out fractures. Reduces unnecessary imaging.

cards.front: A patient presents with sudden-onset worst headache of my life. What is the diagnosis and workup?
cards.back: Subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) until proven otherwise. Workup: Stat non-contrast CT head (sensitivity ~95% within 6 hours). If CT negative → lumbar puncture (look for xanthochromia).
cards.notes: Most common cause: ruptured berry aneurysm. Risk factors: polycystic kidney disease, Ehlers-Danlos, smoking, hypertension.

cards.front: What is the Wells score used for?
cards.back: Clinical prediction rule for estimating probability of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or pulmonary embolism (PE). Categorizes patients as low, moderate, or high probability.
cards.notes: Low probability + negative D-dimer → DVT/PE effectively ruled out. High probability → proceed directly to imaging (ultrasound for DVT, CT angiography for PE).

cards.front: What is the standard treatment for acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI)?
cards.back: Primary PCI (percutaneous coronary intervention) within 90 minutes of first medical contact (door-to-balloon time). If PCI not available within 120 min → fibrinolysis. Also: Aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitor, heparin, statin, β-blocker, ACE inhibitor.
cards.notes: Mnemonic for acute MI: MONA B (Morphine, O2 if needed, Nitroglycerin, Aspirin, Beta-blocker)

cards.front: What electrolyte abnormalities are seen in tumor lysis syndrome?
cards.back: Hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, and hypocalcemia (secondary to calcium-phosphate precipitation). May lead to acute kidney injury.
cards.notes: Prevention: IV fluids + allopurinol or rasburicase. Common after treatment of high-grade lymphomas and leukemias.

cards.front: A child presents with barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and low-grade fever. Diagnosis and treatment?
cards.back: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis), most commonly caused by parainfluenza virus. Treatment: Single dose of dexamethasone. Nebulized epinephrine for moderate-severe cases.
cards.notes: Steeple sign on AP neck X-ray. Peak incidence: 6 months to 3 years. Must differentiate from epiglottitis (thumbprint sign, drooling, toxic-appearing).

cards.front: What are the CURB-65 criteria for pneumonia severity?
cards.back: C: Confusion (new), U: Urea >7 mmol/L (BUN >19), R: Respiratory rate ≥30, B: Blood pressure (SBP <90 or DBP ≤60), 65: Age ≥65. Score ≥2 → consider hospital admission. Score ≥3 → consider ICU.
cards.notes: Alternative: Pneumonia Severity Index (PSI/PORT score). CURB-65 is simpler for clinical use.

cards.front: What is the initial management of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)?
cards.back: 1) Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (normal saline), 2) IV insulin drip, 3) Potassium replacement (if K+ <5.3, replete before insulin), 4) Monitor glucose, electrolytes, anion gap q1-2h.
cards.notes: Do NOT give insulin if K+ <3.3 (risk of fatal hypokalemia). Switch to D5 when glucose <200-250. Bridge to SubQ insulin before stopping drip.

cards.front: A 28-year-old female presents with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum, and non-caseating granulomas on biopsy. Diagnosis?
cards.back: Sarcoidosis. Non-caseating granulomas are the hallmark. Can affect virtually any organ.
cards.notes: Labs: elevated ACE level, hypercalcemia (1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D from macrophages). More common in African American women. Treatment: systemic corticosteroids for significant organ involvement.

cards.front: What are the indications for emergent dialysis?
cards.back: AEIOU: Acidosis (refractory metabolic), Electrolytes (refractory hyperkalemia), Ingestion (toxic alcohols, lithium, salicylates), Overload (refractory fluid/pulmonary edema), Uremia (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding).
cards.notes: These are situations where conservative management has failed or the condition is life-threatening.

cards.front: How do you differentiate prerenal, intrinsic renal, and postrenal AKI?
cards.back: Prerenal: FENa <1%, BUN/Cr >20:1, concentrated urine. Intrinsic (ATN): FENa >2%, BUN/Cr <15:1, muddy brown casts. Postrenal: Hydronephrosis on ultrasound, bladder distension.
cards.notes: FENa may be unreliable if patient is on diuretics — use FEUrea instead (<35% = prerenal).

cards.front: What scoring system is used for risk stratification in atrial fibrillation, and what does it determine?
cards.back: CHA₂DS₂-VASc score determines stroke risk and need for anticoagulation. C: CHF (1), H: HTN (1), A₂: Age ≥75 (2), D: DM (1), S₂: Stroke/TIA (2), V: Vascular disease (1), A: Age 65-74 (1), Sc: Sex category female (1).
cards.notes: Score ≥2 in men, ≥3 in women → anticoagulate (DOAC preferred over warfarin). Score 0 → no anticoagulation needed.

cards.front: A patient with cirrhosis presents with confusion and asterixis. Diagnosis and treatment?
cards.back: Hepatic encephalopathy. Treatment: 1) Lactulose (titrate to 2-3 soft stools/day), 2) Rifaximin (adjunctive for recurrent episodes). Also identify and treat precipitants (GI bleed, infection, constipation, medications).
cards.notes: Lactulose converts NH₃ to NH₄⁺ in the gut (trapped and excreted). Rifaximin reduces ammonia-producing gut bacteria.
